questions.question: What is the theoretical heading accuracy of a GNSS compass with a 4 m baseline between antennas?

answerOptions.rationale: Using the formula $\text{Heading accuracy} \approx \frac{0.229}{L}$, with L = 4 m: 0.229 / 4 = 0.057 deg, approximately 0.06 deg.
0.23 deg corresponds to a 1 m baseline, not 4 m.
0.02 deg corresponds to a 10 m baseline.
0.11 deg corresponds to a 2 m baseline.

questions.hint: The formula relates carrier phase noise to baseline length: $\approx \frac{0.229}{L}$ degrees.

questions.question: During a static AHRS calibration alongside, C-O values are stable. However, during dynamic logging they show a sinusoidal heading-dependent pattern. What is the most likely cause?

answerOptions.text: A latency mismatch between the GNSS reference and the vessel sensor
answerOptions.rationale: A heading-dependent sinusoidal C-O pattern during dynamic conditions, absent in static conditions, is the classic signature of a latency mismatch between two heading systems.

answerOptions.text: Incorrect latitude entered in the gyrocompass
answerOptions.rationale: An incorrect latitude would produce a constant heading bias, not a sinusoidal heading-dependent pattern.
Multipath causes noise and occasional integer ambiguity failures, not a systematic sinusoidal pattern correlated with heading.
MRU mounting issues affect pitch and roll, not the heading-dependent sinusoidal pattern described.

questions.hint: Think about what changes between static and dynamic conditions that could cause a heading-correlated error.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of common control points required between each total station setup during a dimensional control survey?

answerOptions.rationale: A minimum of 5 common control points must be visible from each setup to provide sufficient redundancy for transformation and outlier detection.
3 points define a transformation but provide no redundancy for outlier detection.
7 is more than the minimum requirement, though more points are always beneficial.
4 points provide minimal redundancy. The procedure requires a minimum of 5.

questions.hint: Redundancy is needed for both transformation accuracy and outlier detection.

questions.question: The heave high-pass filter period on your MRU is set to 10 seconds, but the dominant swell period is 12 seconds. What will happen to the bathymetric data?

answerOptions.text: Soundings will be systematically too deep in troughs and too shallow on crests
answerOptions.rationale: When the filter period is shorter than the swell period, the MRU attenuates real heave signal. The under-compensated heave causes depth errors that are too deep in troughs and too shallow on crests.

answerOptions.text: Soundings will have random noise added uniformly
answerOptions.rationale: This is a systematic error correlated with swell, not random noise.

answerOptions.text: No effect, as the 2-second difference is within tolerance
answerOptions.rationale: The rule requires the filter period to exceed the swell period by at least 20%. A 10 s filter for 12 s swell attenuates the signal.

answerOptions.text: The heave signal will be amplified, causing exaggerated depths
answerOptions.rationale: A high-pass filter attenuates signals below its cutoff; it does not amplify them.

questions.hint: The filter period rule states it must exceed the dominant swell period by at least 20%.

questions.question: What is the correct order for the first three calibrations in the mobilisation calibration sequence?

answerOptions.text: Static GNSS intersystem check, AHRS calibration, Gyro comparison
answerOptions.rationale: Positioning must be established first (GNSS check), then heading/attitude verified (AHRS), then individual gyro comparison. Each step depends on the accuracy of the previous one.

answerOptions.text: MBES patch test, USBL calibration, AHRS calibration
answerOptions.rationale: MBES patch test comes last (step 6) because it depends on all positioning and attitude being calibrated first.

answerOptions.text: Gyro comparison, AHRS calibration, Static GNSS check
answerOptions.rationale: This is the reverse order. GNSS positioning must be verified before heading systems can be calibrated against it.

answerOptions.text: USBL calibration, SVP cast, MBES patch test
answerOptions.rationale: These are steps 4-6. The GNSS, AHRS, and gyro checks must come first.

questions.hint: Each calibration step depends on the accuracy of the previous one. What must be established first?

questions.question: A spinning mass gyrocompass has accuracy $\pm 0.2^\circ \times \sec(\text{latitude})$. What is its accuracy at 60 degrees latitude?

answerOptions.rationale: $\sec(60^\circ) = \frac{1}{\cos(60^\circ)} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2.0$, so accuracy = $\pm 0.2 \times 2.0 = \pm 0.4$ deg.
This is the accuracy only at the equator where sec(0) = 1.
This would require cos(latitude) rather than sec(latitude) in the formula.
This does not correspond to the correct calculation of $0.2 \times \sec(60^\circ)$.

questions.hint: Secant is the reciprocal of cosine. What is $\cos(60^\circ)$?

questions.question: During demobilisation, what must be completed before disconnecting any equipment?

answerOptions.text: All data must be backed up to at least two independent locations and verified
answerOptions.rationale: The cardinal rule of demob is to verify all data is backed up and confirmed before disconnecting anything. Once a system is powered down, recovery of incomplete backups may be impossible.

answerOptions.text: All calibration reports must be finalised
answerOptions.rationale: Reports are important but come in Phase 2. Data backup in Phase 1 is non-negotiable before any disconnection.

answerOptions.text: Equipment condition reports must be written for each sensor
answerOptions.rationale: Condition reports are part of documentation (Phase 2), not the first priority before disconnection.
Informing the bridge is important but not the cardinal rule. Data protection comes first.

questions.hint: Data loss during demob is irreversible.

questions.question: An MRU is mounted 10 m forward of the MBES transducer. In 3 degrees of pitch, what is the approximate lever-arm-induced heave?

answerOptions.rationale: Using $\text{Induced heave} = L_x \times \sin(\text{pitch})$: $10 \times \sin(3^\circ) = 10 \times 0.0523 = 0.52$ m.
0.17 m corresponds to a 10 m lever arm at only 1 degree of pitch.
0.26 m corresponds to a 5 m lever arm at 3 degrees of pitch.
1.05 m corresponds to a 20 m lever arm at 3 degrees of pitch.

questions.question: What settling time must be allowed for a spinning mass gyrocompass before beginning calibration?

answerOptions.rationale: Spinning mass gyrocompasses require 4 to 6 hours from a cold start to reach their quoted accuracy.
20-30 minutes is the settling time for a fibre optic gyrocompass (FOG), not a spinning mass type.
Less than 5 minutes applies to ring laser gyrocompasses (RLG).
1-2 hours is insufficient. Spinning mass gyros need 4-6 hours.

questions.hint: Spinning mass gyros use a mechanical rotor that must physically settle, taking much longer than electronic gyros.

questions.question: During a dimensional control survey, if the opening and closing observations to a control point differ by what threshold, must all observations from that setup be re-observed?

answerOptions.rationale: The 2 mm rule: if the difference between opening and closing observation to a control point is 2 mm or more, all observations from that setup must be re-observed.
5 mm is too lenient. The threshold is 2 mm.
1 mm is tighter than the specified threshold of 2 mm.
0.5 mm is the RMS threshold for plane and circle fit residuals, not for control point closure.

questions.hint: This is a critical quality check performed before and after each total station setup.

questions.question: When using the tape measure DC method, what is the maximum recommended distance for reliable offset measurements?

answerOptions.rationale: Beyond 5 m, tape sag and path errors make measurements unreliable. A total station should be used instead.
3 m is within the reliable range but is not the upper limit. Tape is valid up to 5 m.
10 m far exceeds the reliable range for tape measure dimensional control.
1 m is well within the reliable range. The actual limit is 5 m.

questions.hint: Consider the effects of tape sag and path errors on longer measurements.

questions.question: In the vessel coordinate reference system for a dimensional control survey, what is the standard positive Z-axis direction?

answerOptions.rationale: In the vessel DC survey coordinate system, the CRP is at X=0, Y=0, Z=0 with positive Z pointing upwards, based on the vessel baseline.
Downwards is the positive Z convention used for some ROV body frames (X-forward, Y-starboard, Z-down), but the vessel DC survey uses positive-up.
Forward is the Y-axis direction in the vessel DC survey reference frame.
Starboard is the positive X-axis direction in the vessel DC survey reference frame.

questions.hint: The vessel DC survey uses the baseline as the Z reference, with CRP at the aft perpendicular on the centreline.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of delayed heave (True Heave) over real-time heave for bathymetric deliverables?

answerOptions.text: It processes data in both forward and reverse directions, eliminating phase errors and amplitude attenuation
answerOptions.rationale: Delayed heave buffers approximately 150 seconds of data and processes using a non-causal filter. This removes the phase and amplitude errors inherent in real-time causal filtering, especially for long-period swell.

answerOptions.text: It uses a different type of accelerometer with higher accuracy
answerOptions.rationale: Delayed heave uses the same sensor data as real-time heave. The improvement comes from the processing method, not the hardware.

answerOptions.text: It removes the need for lever arm corrections
answerOptions.rationale: Lever arm corrections are still needed regardless of whether real-time or delayed heave is used.

answerOptions.text: It replaces MRU heave entirely with GNSS height
answerOptions.rationale: Delayed heave still uses MRU acceleration data. GNSS-aided heave is a separate method.

questions.hint: Think about the difference between causal (real-time) and non-causal (post-processed) filtering.

questions.question: During the static AHRS intersystem check, what is the maximum acceptable heading difference between any two systems per IMCA S 017?

answerOptions.rationale: Per IMCA S 017, the heading tolerance between any two systems during a static intersystem check is 0.5 deg or less.
1.0 deg exceeds the acceptance criterion. If the difference is this large, investigation is required.
0.1 deg is the tolerance for pitch and roll differences, not heading.
0.3 deg is the AHRS calibration tolerance for GNSS reference vs GNSS/INS heading, not the intersystem check threshold.

questions.hint: This criterion comes from IMCA S 017 guidelines for position reference systems.

questions.question: Why must the total station tilt compensator be switched OFF when performing a dimensional control survey on a vessel deck?

answerOptions.text: Corrections depend on where in the pitch/roll cycle the observation is made, introducing random error
answerOptions.rationale: On a dynamic platform, the tilt compensator applies corrections at arbitrary points in the pitch/roll cycle. This introduces random error rather than correcting for instrument levelling.

answerOptions.text: The compensator cannot handle angles greater than 1 degree
answerOptions.rationale: Modern tilt compensators can handle several degrees of tilt. The issue is the dynamic nature of the motion, not the magnitude.

answerOptions.text: It slows down the measurement rate
answerOptions.rationale: The compensator operates in real-time and does not significantly affect measurement speed.

answerOptions.text: It consumes too much battery power
answerOptions.rationale: Power consumption is not the reason. The issue is accuracy degradation on a moving platform.

questions.hint: Consider what happens when the vessel is constantly moving through its pitch and roll cycle during each observation.
